items.title: Hamilton judge rules Ontario's sex offender registry is unconstitutional. What could happen next?
items.summary_auto: The decision is "significant" and could see changes to Ontario’s sex offender registry, depending on how the appeals process plays out, says the applicant's lawyer. The province says it plans to appeal.

items.title: Canadian man stuck in ICE detention centre for 4 months, awaiting deportation hearing
items.summary_auto: Since November, Edmonton-born Curtis Wright has been stuck in an ICE detainment facility in Texas. Since then, his family has been pushing for his release while his case moves through the courts.

items.title: U.S. terrorism case against former Ont. student includes 'classified' national security material
items.summary_auto: U.S. prosecutors have taken steps to protect classified national security material set to be part of their case against a Pakistani man accused of plotting an attack on Jews in New York. Muhammad Shahzeb Khan, who previously lived in the Toronto area, is scheduled to stand trial in May.

items.title: What's the difference between a public inquiry and coroner's inquest into Tumbler Ridge?
items.summary_auto: A coroner's inquest has been called into the Tumbler Ridge mass shooting and B.C. 's public safety minister says a public inquiry is also possible. But how do the two differ and what can Canadians expect out of them?

items.title: Fact check: Grok tells users fake Tel Aviv video is real
items.summary_auto: Can you tell this video is fake? The AI chatbot Grok could not. It’s been viewed millions of times on platforms like X and Instagram, with claims it shows Iranian missiles hitting Tel Aviv.

items.title: IN PHOTOS | War in Iran enters 5th day
items.summary_auto: Israel and the U.S. launched a new wave of strikes on Iran on Wednesday, prompting more missiles and drones fired from Tehran toward Israel. Turkey became the latest country to be drawn into the conflict, after NATO defence systems destroyed an Iranian ballistic missile on its way to Turkish airspace.

items.title: Mexico seeks to dispel lingering fears in Guadalajara ahead of World Cup
items.summary_auto: World Cup preparations in Guadalajara, one of three host Mexican cities, were recently plunged under a cloud of uncertainty following an explosion of violence on Feb. 22 that ripped through the city, across Jalisco, and flared in several parts of the country.

items.title: She was set to win the U.S. women's half marathon. Then a guide car made a wrong turn
items.summary_auto: Jessica McClain was ahead by a wide margin with about 2.4 kilometres left to go at the U.S. half marathon championships when she and three other runners followed a guide vehicle on a wrong turn, leading them off-course for about one kilometre.

items.title: Carney shakes up senior ranks of the public service
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney has shaken up the senior ranks of the public service, creating and expanding some roles in a continued effort to put his stamp on the machinery of government.

items.title: Ontario students stage mass protest over Ford government’s cuts to aid program
items.summary_auto: Students across the province are protesting recent changes to the Ontario Student Assistance Program (OSAP) — the post-secondary financial aid system will go from a majority grant structure to a majority loan structure in the new school year.

items.title: Judge grants bail to one of four accused in London, Ont., explosives case
items.summary_auto: Zekun Wang, who also goes by Alex, appeared at the London courthouse on Wednesday, where Justice of the Peace Kristine Diaz granted him bail. He's one of four people accused of possessing high explosives and manufacturing firearms, following an investigation in January.

items.title: Moroun, Ambassador Bridge company turned to ex-Harper aides’ firm to lobby Canadian officials
items.summary_auto: The billionaire U.S. owners of the busiest land border crossing in North America turned to a high-powered lobbying firm run by former top aides to Prime Minister Stephen Harper in recent years, records show.

items.title: 3 million Canadians use GLP-1 drugs. Survey says that's changing their appetites — and habits
items.summary_auto: A new survey suggests about three million Canadian adults are currently taking GLP-1 drugs such as Ozempic or Mounjaro and that many more would like to, but cost is a barrier.

items.title: Curling rocks stolen from Milano-Cortina Paralympic venue ahead of wheelchair competition
items.summary_auto: Curling drama has arrived at the Paralympics even before the first competition rock has been thrown. Two curling stones have been stolen from the Cortina Curling Olympic Stadium.

items.title: Sister First Nations in Ontario, Manitoba speak out against mining exploration underway
items.summary_auto: Two Anisininew Nations in northwestern Ontario and northeastern Manitoba are speaking out against mining exploration in their traditional territories, which they say is happening without their consent. Leaders from Sachigo Lake First Nation and Red Sucker Lake First Nation held a joint press conference at Queen’s Park on Tuesday about the Lingman Lake Gold Project. Here are their concerns and how Signature Resources Ltd. has responded to them.

items.title: Brian Da Costa, alleged drug trafficker accused of bribing Toronto cops, gets $1.5M bail
items.summary_auto: The alleged drug trafficker accused of obtaining confidential information from a Toronto police officer, which was used in an alleged conspiracy to kill a senior corrections officer, was granted a $1.5-million bail Wednesday in Toronto.

items.title: Frank Stronach says 'justice will prevail' in sexual assault trial as 7th and final complainant cross-examined
items.summary_auto: Canadian businessman Frank Stronach said that "justice will prevail" in his sexual assault trial being held in a Toronto courthouse.

items.title: Vancouver teacher says sudden licence suspension over email sent to spam felt like 'gut punch'
items.summary_auto: Dozens of Lower Mainland teachers were suspended after emails from the Ministry of Education about updating their criminal record checks ended up in their spam inboxes. Now, those teachers want to know if the ministry will compensate them for the days they weren't allowed to teach.

items.title: The U.S. is further along in its AI data centre buildout. How does Alberta's approach compare?
items.summary_auto: While the U.S. has developed far more AI infrastructure, Alberta also sees a major opportunity to capitalize on the AI boom, given  its colder climate, vast real estate and deregulated electricity market.

items.title: U.S. submarine torpedoes Iranian ship in Indian Ocean, reportedly killing at least 87 sailors
items.summary_auto: A U.S. submarine sank a warship with a torpedo off the southern coast of Sri ​Lanka, dramatically widening Washington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.

items.title: Calgary's WinSport again defends demolishing bobsled track after athlete criticism
items.summary_auto: WinSport is again defending demolishing the bobsled and luge track at Canada Olympic Park in 2019, having most recently received criticism after Canada’s sledding athletes missed the Olympic podium for the first time in 24 years.

items.title: B.C. cities can set their own time zones, province says
items.summary_auto: As B.C. 's provincial government does away with biannual time changes, the East Kootenay region will continue to spring forward and fall back with Alberta. And, the province says, other local governments can choose to set their own time zones, if they want to.

items.title: Trump admin hands legal victory to alleged B.C. defeat device smugglers
items.summary_auto: Two B.C. men were staring at up to 20 years in prison for allegedly attempting to subvert U.S. clean air laws by smuggling millions of dollars worth of illegal car parts across the border. Then U.S. President Donald Trump’s administration made a U-turn on environmental law enforcement.

items.title: Mike Myers, Hazel Mae among recipients of special 2026 Canadian Screen Awards honours
items.summary_auto: Canadian Screen Week takes place in Toronto from May 27 to May 31, culminating with the Canadian Screen Awards ceremony honouring the best in the country's cinema, television and digital media.

items.title: Polar bears aren't hunting people, they're just out of ice: Scientists
items.summary_auto: "Nutritional stress does play a role. It's just that it doesn't appear to be the role that we thought it was, just driving bears en masse desperately into communities," said Douglas Clark, a professor in the University of Saskatchewan’s School of Environment and Sustainability.

items.title: N.S. bill cloaks identity of children who die in care from public
items.summary_auto: A new bill before the Nova Scotia Legislature is making changes to the way publication bans guard the identity of a child in the care of the province — keeping them in place even if that child dies.

items.title: Time running out in Canada for man who caused Humboldt Broncos bus crash
items.summary_auto: Jaskirat Singh Sidhu, the man responsible for the 2018 Humboldt Broncos bus crash, could be deported to India within weeks, leaving behind a wife and two children who are pleading with Canada to let him stay. At the same time, some families of the victims say Sidhu should have been removed from Canada years ago.

items.title: Hamilton police officer, investigated for sharing racist content, displays sticker associated with white nationalist extremism, group says
items.summary_auto: A Hamilton community group is calling for action after it says a resident discovered a police officer's vehicle is once again displaying a sticker “associated with white nationalist extremism." Police had recently investigated the officer for sharing racist and extremist content on social media.

items.title: He holds the record for most days skied at Mont Tremblant — and uses status to support others
items.summary_auto: Ken Bull holds the overall record for most days skied in a season at Mont Tremblant set in 2014. He was at the top of the leaderboard for last season as well until a recent re-count placed him in third. He uses his celebrity to update the community on a local skier who suffered a devastating accident in training

items.title: Family seeking $1.3M owed by Alberta separatist leader Dennis Modry after court order
items.summary_auto: Relatives of a prominent Alberta separatist who met with U.S. officials are upset that he has failed to pay back more than $1.3 million that he owes from a court order issued almost a year ago. A B.C. Supreme Court judge ruled in March 2025 that Dennis Modry, a co-founder of the Alberta Prosperity Project, misappropriated the money from the joint bank account of his aunt and uncle.

items.title: B.C. is moving to permanent daylight time. Could your province be next?
items.summary_auto: British Columbia’s move to permanent daylight time has reignited conversations across the country about scrapping biannual time changes. After B.C. Premier David Eby announced Monday that most of the province will change its clocks for the last time this weekend, Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said it’s time to consider following suit. Other provinces say discussions are happening.

items.title: Months after suspension for racist posts, Hamilton officer's car bears sticker with symbol ‘appropriated by’ neo-Nazis
items.summary_auto: A Hamilton community group is calling for action after it says a resident discovered a police officer's vehicle is once again displaying a sticker “associated with white nationalist extremism." Police had recently investigated the officer for sharing racist and extremist content on social media.

items.title: How long can Iran keep firing missiles and drones?
items.summary_auto: Experts say Tehran will likely try to inflict collateral damage in neighbouring countries and exhaust U.S. and Israeli defences by using cheap kamikaze drones in order to try and save the regime.

items.title: As Iran war raises terror risk, U.S. enforcement agencies are spread thin, experts say
items.summary_auto: After a year of upheaval, departures and shifting priorities at some of the U.S. agencies charged with keeping the U.S. safe, experts say the U.S. may be more vulnerable to a terror attack. The conflict with Iran also comes as hundreds of Homeland Security personnel are not being paid or have been furloughed because of the government shutdown.

items.title: Why researchers say lowering Canada's radon limit could prevent more cases of lung cancer and save lives
items.summary_auto: Could a lower threshold for radon levels in Canadian homes protect more people from getting lung cancer? Some researchers say Canada is overdue for an evidence-backed rethink in light of emerging research and rising risks.

items.title: 'It's not easy, let's be clear': Carney speaks frankly about dealing with Trump at an event in Australia
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney pulled back the curtain on his relationship with his American counterpart on Wednesday evening in Australia, saying it's not easy dealing with U.S. President Donald Trump and he must choose his words carefully to avoid any blowback.

items.title: Who will lead Iran now that Khamenei is gone? It's not clear
items.summary_auto: In the days since the surprise death of Iran's supreme leader at the hands of the U.S. and Israel Saturday, there has been much speculation — and confusion — about who's in charge and who will succeed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
